The believing hope and prospect of eternal life should make us steady and constant in our Christian lives. There is a difference in gifts and graces, yet, because we are renewed by the same Spirit, we are brothers and sisters. To stand fast in the Lord is to stand fast in his strength, and by his grace.

Let believers be of one mind and ready to help each other. As the apostle had found benefit from their assistance, he knew how comforting it would be to his fellow workers to have the help of others. Let us seek to give assurance that our names are written in the book of life. Joy in God is very important in the Christian life; and Christians need to be reminded of it again and again. It outweighs all causes for sorrow. Let their enemies see how moderate they were regarding outward things, and how calmly they suffered loss and hardships. The day of judgment will soon arrive, with full redemption for believers and destruction for ungodly people. There is a care of diligence which is our duty, and agrees with wise planning and due concern; but there is a care of fear and distrust, which is sin and folly, and only perplexes and distracts the mind. As a remedy against troubling cares, constant prayer is recommended. Not only at set times for prayer, but in everything by prayer. We must join thanksgivings with prayers and supplications; not only seek supplies of good things, but acknowledge the mercies we have received. God does not need to be told our wants or desires; he knows them better than we do; but he wants us to show that we value his mercy and feel our dependence on him. The peace of God, the comfortable sense of being reconciled to God, having a part in his favor, and the hope of heavenly blessedness, are a greater good than can be fully expressed. This peace will keep our hearts and minds through Christ Jesus; it will keep us from sinning under troubles, and from sinking under them; keep us calm and with inward satisfaction. Believers are to get and keep a good name; a reputation for goodness with God and good people. We should walk in all the ways of virtue, and abide therein; then, whether our praise is from people or not, it will be from God. The apostle is an example. His teaching and life agreed together. The way to have the God of peace with us is to stay close to our duty. All our privileges and salvation arise from the free mercy of God; yet the enjoyment of them depends on our sincere and holy conduct. These are works of God, pertaining to God, and to him only are they to be ascribed, and to no other, neither people, words, nor deeds.

It is a good work to help a good minister in trouble. The nature of true Christian sympathy is not only to feel concern for our friends in their troubles, but to do what we can to help them. The apostle was often imprisoned and in need; but in all these situations, he learned to be content, to accept his circumstances, and make the best of it. Pride, unbelief, a desire for things we don’t have, and dissatisfaction with present blessings, make people discontented even when they are doing well. Let us pray for patient submission and hope when we are humbled; for humility and a heavenly mind when we are honored. It is a special grace to have an even temper of mind always. And in times of hardship not to lose our comfort in God, nor distrust his care, nor take any wrong course for our own support. In prosperous conditions not to be proud, or secure, or focused on worldly things. This is a harder lesson than the other; because the temptations of fullness and prosperity are greater than those of affliction and want. The apostle had no intention of urging them to give more, but to encourage kindness that will receive a glorious reward later. Through Christ we have grace to do what is good, and through him we must expect the reward; and as we have all things by him, let us do all things for him, and to his glory.

The apostle ends with praises to God. We should look upon God, under all our weakness and fears, not as an enemy, but as a Father, disposed to pity us and help us. We must give glory to God as a Father. God’s grace and favor, which reconciled souls enjoy, along with all the graces within us that flow from it, are all purchased for us by Christ's merit, and applied by his pleading for us; and therefore are justly called the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ.
